purpose: Draft (inactive) Chapter 4 question objects derived from the Water Survival Student Manual excerpt (pages 89-119, missing ~page 118) processed in vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md. These are NOT part of src/content/course.json's live "questions" array and will not appear in the app until explicitly promoted. Tier assignments (1/2/3) live in vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-promotion-review.md, not in this file.
activationRule: Do not add any of these questions to course.json unless its verificationStatus has been changed away from 'draft-unverified' as part of an explicitly-requested promotion task.
sourceHandlingNotes: Excludes: the immersion suit thermal-protection figure (manual says 2-5 degrees C over 6 hours; the already-live CFR-sourced content states a 2C cap -- flagged as a discrepancy in the source intake, not drafted here to avoid contradicting the live Immersion Suits module). Excludes: the Williamson Turn's second-phase degree arithmetic (the source intake's worked example, '310 degrees', does not check out against its own stated rule -- only the turn's general purpose/first-phase concept is drafted, with no degree arithmetic at all). Excludes: any content attributed to the missing ~page 118 (nothing was invented to fill that gap). Excludes: Marine Life/shark-and-jellyfish content -- present in the source folder but not written up in detail in the source intake document, so not used as a citable fact base here. One question (hypothermia onset timing) is phrased as a conservative true/false that holds under either of the source's two onset-timing framings, rather than picking one specific number.

questions.prompt: When assuming the proper stepping-off position before jumping into the water, what should you do with one hand?

choices.text: Hold a signaling whistle to your mouth
choices.misconception: Invents a signaling action that isn't part of the stepping-off position; the hand's job here is protecting your airway on impact.

choices.text: Grip a flashlight to signal your position mid-fall
choices.misconception: Invents an unrelated action not described as part of the stepping-off sequence.

choices.text: Cover your eyes to protect them from splash
choices.misconception: Not part of the described stepping-off sequence; the sequence specifically calls for blocking the nose and mouth, not the eyes.

hints: Think about what could go wrong with your airway on a hard water impact, separate from what your other hand is doing (gripping your lifejacket across your chest).
questions.explanation: The stepping-off position calls for blocking your nose and mouth with one hand, while the other hand crosses your chest gripping your lifejacket firmly -- protecting your airway during the impact of entering the water.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Assume the Stepping Off Position section (manual page ~94); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md. Note: distinct from, though compatible with, the already-live abandon-ship jump-procedure question, which covers a different detail of the same overall sequence (crossed arms/legs and checking for debris below).

questions.prompt: If caught in a rip or dangerous current while swimming, what should you do instead of swimming directly against it?

choices.text: Swim parallel to the shore, or diagonally across the current, rather than fighting it head-on
Stop swimming entirely and let the current carry you until it weakens on its own
choices.misconception: Understates the recommended response -- the guidance is an active but smarter swimming strategy, not passive drifting.

choices.text: Swim directly with the current, away from shore, until you're clear of it
choices.misconception: Overstates moving away from shore as the goal, when the actual guidance is about working at an angle toward safety, not abandoning progress toward shore.

choices.text: Dive to the bottom and push off toward shore
choices.misconception: Diving to the bottom isn't the described technique -- the guidance is about swim direction relative to the current, not depth.

hints: Fighting a strong current head-on wastes energy without making progress -- what direction actually works?
questions.explanation: In a rip or dangerous current, don't fight it head-on. Swim parallel to the shore, or diagonally across the current, and only head toward shore once you're clear of the current's pull.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Swimming for Survival section (manual page ~95); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md. Note: this confirms the general escape-direction strategy from the source; the already-live 45-degree-angle question is a separate, more specific figure not independently confirmed in this excerpt -- see the promotion review's near-duplicate discussion before promoting.

questions.prompt: True or False: hypothermia in cold water can begin developing in well under an hour.

choices.misconception: The source material describes onset beginning in well under an hour under both of its stated framings (roughly ten to fifteen minutes in one passage, up to about thirty minutes in another), so 'well under an hour' holds either way.

hints: This question is phrased broadly on purpose -- think about whether either of the specific timeframes you may have learned would make this statement true.
questions.explanation: Hypothermia onset can begin well under an hour into cold-water immersion. Exact timing varies by water temperature, wind chill, clothing, body fat, and activity level -- treat any specific number as a rough guide dependent on conditions, not a fixed rule.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Cold Water Survival / Hypothermia section (manual pages ~101, ~104); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md. Note: the source intake flagged two different onset-timing figures in this section (roughly 10-15 minutes to several hours in one passage; up to 30 minutes in the Cold Shock Response chart) that were not reconciled. This question is deliberately phrased to hold true under either reading rather than picking one specific number.

questions.prompt: If you need to find fresh water after reaching shore, roughly where should you dig, and how should you approach it?

choices.text: Roughly 100 yards above the high-tide line, digging only about a foot past where water first seeps in and scooping from the top, since fresh water floats above salt water in the sand
As close to the waterline as possible, digging as deep as you can before stopping
choices.misconception: Reverses both the recommended digging location (well above the high-tide line, not near the waterline) and the guidance to stop soon after water appears, not dig as deep as possible.

choices.text: Anywhere on the beach at random, since fresh water is equally likely to be found everywhere near shore
choices.misconception: Ignores the specific location guidance given (well above the high-tide line); treating the location as random misses the described method entirely.

choices.text: Only directly beneath visible vegetation, since that is the sole reliable indicator given
choices.misconception: Overstates a single indicator as the only reliable method, when the source's guidance is about digging location and technique, not exclusively about vegetation.

hints: Fresh water is lighter than salt water and tends to sit above it in the sand -- think about what that means for how deep you should dig.
questions.explanation: Dig roughly 100 yards above the high-tide line, stopping about a foot past where water first seeps in and scooping from the top -- fresh water floats above salt water in the sand, so digging too deep risks reaching salt water instead. Try another spot if the water is still too salty.
questions.sourceReference: Chapter 4 manual source intake, Finding Food and Water ashore section (manual page ~119); see src/content/imports/vinci-2026-07-13-chapter-4-manual-source-intake.md. Note: this content immediately follows the confirmed-missing ~page 118, and the section's own opening context is uncertain -- see the promotion review for why this is held rather than promotion-ready despite the specific technique itself being clearly legible on the available page.

promotionHistory.note: Promotion of the 40 Tier 1 questions from the Chapter 4 manual-derived draft pack. 3 Tier 2 questions (steppingoff-blocknose, ripcurrent-escape, hypothermia-onset-conservative) and 1 Tier 3 question (finding-water-ashore-digging, missing-page-adjacent) remain in this file, inactive, pending future light review / the missing page 118.
